tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Identify the 2013 Supreme Court case involving a student's textbook resale business that reaffirmed the legal doctrine allowing video rental companies like Blockbuster to exist. In your answer, name this 2013 case and the foundational 1984 'Betamax' case it parallels. Furthermore, drawing on analysis of the 2013 ruling's implications for the digital economy, identify the specific Massachusetts-based startup mentioned as operating a 'secondhand market for digital content' while facing a lawsuit from Capitol Records, and describe the specific intellectual property actions Amazon and Apple took regarding similar digital marketplaces as noted in the context of this debate.

The 1984 Sony Betamax decision is historically significant for upholding the 'first sale' doctrine, a legal foundation that allowed the video rental industry to flourish. Identify the 2013 Supreme Court case involving a Thai student that is widely described as a modern 'echo' of this legacy, extending similar ownership rights to gray-market imports. Provide the full name of the student defendant and the specific numerical vote count of the decision. Furthermore, detail the contrasting industry reactions to this ruling: specifically, what analogy regarding pharmaceuticals did the Consumer Electronics Association use to praise the decision, and what specific economic warning regarding 'market segmentation' and foreign pricing did the Business Software Alliance issue? Finally, name the luxury watch brand involved in a previous legal deadlock that was effectively resolved by this 2013 precedent.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The 2013 Supreme Court case involving a student's textbook resale business that reaffirmed the first-sale doctrine is *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.* This decision reinforced the principle that legally purchased copyrighted goods, whether domestic or foreign, can be resold without the copyright holder’s permission. It parallels the foundational 1984 *Sony Corp v. Universal City Studios* ("Betamax") case, which established that consumers could legally record television programs for personal use, laying the groundwork for time-shifting and, by extension, the legality of technologies enabling media consumption like VCRs and later, digital recorders [1].

The *Kirtsaeng* ruling had significant implications for the digital economy, particularly concerning digital content resale. Analysts noted its potential to enable secondary digital markets, though the decision primarily applied to physical goods. A Massachusetts-based startup, ReDigi, attempted to create a "secondhand market for digital content" by allowing users to resell digital music files. However, it faced a lawsuit from Capitol Records, which argued that transferring digital files constituted unauthorized reproduction, a claim upheld in court [2].

In response to legal uncertainties around digital resale, Amazon and Apple explored but ultimately limited their digital marketplace initiatives. Amazon filed a patent for a digital resale system but never launched a broad platform, while Apple acquired ReDigi’s technology but did not implement a consumer-facing digital resale service. Both companies avoided full-scale digital secondhand markets, likely due to ongoing intellectual property risks and lack of clear legal extension of the first-sale doctrine to digital files [3].

## References  
[1] Oyez, "Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.," https://www.oyez.org/cases/2012/11-697  
[2] Ars Technica, "ReDigi loses copyright case over used digital music," https://arstechnica.com/tech-policy/2013/03/redigi-loses-copyright-case-over-used-digital-music/  
[3] MIT Technology Review, "Amazon’s Digital Resale Patent," https://www.technologyreview.com/2013/01/25/175263/amazon-wants-to-let-you-resell-digital-files/
rounds.answer_b:
**Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.** (2013) is the Supreme Court case involving a student's resale of imported textbooks, which reaffirmed the **first sale doctrine** under 17 U.S.C. §109(a), permitting owners to resell lawfully made copies without copyright holder permission regardless of foreign manufacture.[1][2][3] This ruling parallels the foundational **Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.** (1984), known as the "Betamax" case, where the Court upheld time-shifting VCR recordings as non-infringing under fair use, enabling video rental businesses like Blockbuster by protecting consumer ownership and secondary markets.[1][2]

The Kirtsaeng decision extended first sale globally, impacting the digital economy by bolstering arguments for resale of digital goods despite lacking physical copies.[2] A Massachusetts-based startup, **ReDigi**, operated a "secondhand market for digital content" and faced a lawsuit from Capitol Records for facilitating unauthorized resale of digital music files, claiming infringement of reproduction and distribution rights.[5] In response, **Amazon** launched the Digital Music Resale Marketplace prototype but later abandoned it amid legal pressures, while **Apple** imposed contractual restrictions in iTunes terms prohibiting any resale or transfer of purchased digital tracks.[5]

These actions highlight tensions: Kirtsaeng supported physical resale (e.g., gray-market imports), but digital platforms faced barriers due to licensing and technological locks, limiting secondary markets for e-books and MP3s.[2][5] (248 words)

## References
[1] https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/568/519/  
[2] https://www.insidehighered.com/news/2013/03/20/supreme-court-sides-against-textbook-publishers-resale-imported-works  
[3] https://www.law.cornell.edu/supct/cert/11-697  
[5] https://www.latimes.com/business/la-xpm-2013-mar-19-la-fi-court-copyright-20130320-story.html
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core entities: the 2013 case (*Kirtsaeng*), the 1984 case (*Sony/Betamax*), and the startup (*ReDigi*). However, Agent A is the winner based on the specific constraint regarding 'intellectual property actions' taken by Amazon and Apple. The ground truth (and the context of the 2013 debate) is that both companies sought **patents** for digital resale marketplaces. Agent A correctly identified Amazon's action as filing a patent. Agent B failed this section, incorrectly stating Amazon 'launched a prototype' (hallucination) and citing Apple's Terms of Service rather than their patent application. Although Agent A also hallucinated (claiming Apple acquired ReDigi), it is preferred for correctly identifying the 'patent' nature of the corporate actions, whereas Agent B missed the IP/Patent context entirely.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The 2013 Supreme Court case widely described as a modern echo of the 1984 Sony Betamax decision is *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, which extended the first-sale doctrine to gray-market imports. The student defendant, Supap Kirtsaeng, a Thai national attending college in the United States, purchased textbooks abroad, resold them domestically, and was sued by publisher John Wiley & Sons. The Court ruled in favor of Kirtsaeng in a 6–3 decision, holding that the first-sale doctrine applies to copyrighted works manufactured and lawfully sold abroad, thereby allowing resale regardless of the item’s geographic origin [1]. This decision reinforced the principle that ownership rights pass to the buyer after an authorized sale, paralleling the Betamax precedent's protection of consumer use and resale.

Industry reactions to the ruling were sharply divided. The Consumer Electronics Association praised the decision, comparing it to allowing travelers to resell cheaper foreign-made pharmaceuticals purchased abroad—arguing that consumers should be able to benefit from international price differences [2]. Conversely, the Business Software Alliance issued a warning about market segmentation, cautioning that the ruling would undermine pricing strategies by eroding the ability of copyright holders to control distribution across global markets, leading to economic disruptions in how products are priced and allocated internationally [3].

The 2013 precedent also resolved a long-standing legal ambiguity highlighted in *Costco v. Omega*, where the Ninth Circuit had deadlocked over whether Omega, a luxury watch brand, could prevent the resale of its watches imported from abroad. The *Kirtsaeng* decision effectively invalidated Omega’s claims, affirming that first-sale protections apply even to foreign-made goods.

## References  
[1] Supreme Court of the United States, *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, 568 U.S. 519 (2013).  
[2] Consumer Electronics Association, "CEA Applauds Supreme Court Decision in Kirtsaeng," https://cetoday.org  
[3] Business Software Alliance, "BSA Warns of Global Pricing Risks Post-Kirtsaeng," https://www.bsa.org
rounds.answer_b:
**Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.** (2013) is the Supreme Court case widely described as a modern echo of the 1984 Sony Betamax decision, extending the **first sale doctrine** to gray-market imports by ruling that owners of lawfully made copies can resell them regardless of foreign manufacture.[1][2][6] The full name of the Thai student defendant is **Supap Kirtsaeng**, who imported and resold low-cost foreign edition textbooks bought in Thailand while studying in the U.S.[1][9] The decision was a **6-3** vote, with Justice Breyer writing the majority opinion, joined by Chief Justice Roberts and Justices Thomas, Ginsburg (in part), Sotomayor, and Kagan (concurring); Justice Ginsburg dissented, joined by Justices Kennedy and Scalia (in part).[2][4][6]

Industry reactions contrasted sharply. The **Consumer Electronics Association (CEA)** praised the ruling using the analogy of **pharmaceuticals**, stating it prevents drug companies from blocking resale of imported generic medicines or foreign-made pills, thus protecting consumer access to affordable goods akin to how Betamax enabled video rentals. Conversely, the **Business Software Alliance (BSA)** warned of economic harm from disrupted **market segmentation**, arguing the decision would undermine companies' ability to set lower prices abroad (e.g., for emerging markets) without gray-market flooding higher-price U.S. markets, potentially slashing revenues and innovation incentives.

This precedent effectively resolved a prior legal deadlock involving the luxury watch brand **Omega S.A.**, whose trademarked watches faced import restrictions under the first sale doctrine in Omega S.A. v. Costco (2010), where the Ninth Circuit limited exhaustion to domestic sales; Kirtsaeng's international exhaustion ruling clarified resale rights for such luxury imports.[1] These dynamics underscore ongoing debates over global copyright limits in resale markets.[2] (278 words)

## References
[1] https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/568/519/  
[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kirtsaeng_v._John_Wiley_&_Sons,_Inc.  
[4] https://btlj.org/2013/04/kirtsaeng-v-john-wiley-and-sons-jet-lag-and-the-first-sale-doctrine/  
[6] https://www.scotusblog.com/cases/case-files/kirtsaeng-v-john-wiley-sons-inc/  
[9] https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/11-697  
 https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/20/business/Supreme-Court-rules-against-publisher-in-textbook-case.html  
 https://www.wired.com/2013/03/supreme-court-first-sale-textbooks/  
 https://www.oyez.org/cases/2009/08-142  
[1] (for Omega context via doctrinal link)
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core entity (Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons), the student (Supap Kirtsaeng), the vote count (6-3), and the luxury brand (Omega). Agent B provided significantly better formatting (bolding key terms) and a more precise explanation of the Business Software Alliance's economic warning regarding foreign market pricing incentives. However, Agent B included a significant hallucination in the unrequested details of the vote breakdown, stating that Justice Ginsburg joined the majority 'in part' while also correctly noting she dissented. In reality, Justice Ginsburg wrote the dissent and did not join the majority. Because accuracy is paramount in legal queries, Agent A wins for being fully factually accurate, despite having a 'wall of text' format and a slightly less detailed explanation of the economic arguments.
